Bruce Springsteen drew an estimated 200,000 people to a rally in Saint Paul with a performance of his recent single, written about federal law enforcement shootings that have galvanized the opposition movement. Protests were largely peaceful, though Los Angeles saw tear gas deployed near a federal detention facility downtown, and a number of arrests were made in Portland.

The war itself may be approaching a critical inflection point. Markets rallied sharply Tuesday on an unconfirmed report that Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian is open to ending hostilities if certain guarantees can be reached. Earlier in the week, President Trump extended a negotiating deadline, a move the bond market watched closely. If a ceasefire or diplomatic agreement materializes, the downstream effects would be enormous: lower oil prices, easing inflation expectations, and a meaningful drop in Treasury yields. The Strait of Hormuz, through which roughly 20 percent of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas flows, has been effectively restricted since hostilities began in late February. That energy disruption is the single biggest driver of economic volatility right now, and Tuesday's market surge reflects just how much investors are pricing in a potential resolution.

On Capitol Hill, the House passed a stopgap DHS funding bill by a narrow 213 to 203 vote after Republicans rejected the Senate's version of the deal. The standoff means the Department of Homeland Security remains operating on a patchwork funding arrangement, with the federal immigration enforcement apparatus caught in the middle of a weeks-long congressional stalemate. No resolution appears imminent, and the debate over immigration enforcement funding is increasingly entangled with the broader political fallout from the Iran war protests. The CPAC conference this past weekend offered a window into the Republican base's complicated feelings about the war, with some attendees expressing anxiety about a prolonged conflict even as they backed the administration's broader foreign policy posture.

Shifting gears to home lending, the Iran conflict is doing measurable damage to mortgage affordability, and the numbers are worth understanding clearly. The average thirty-year fixed rate hit 6.38 percent this week, up for the fourth consecutive week. Before Operation Epic Fury launched in late February, that same rate was sitting just under 6 percent. That 40-basis-point jump might sound modest in isolation, but in real terms it adds roughly 63 dollars a month to a typical first-time homebuyer's payment. Over the life of a thirty-year loan, that works out to more than 22,000 dollars in additional interest. For buyers who were already stretching on affordability, that is a meaningful and discouraging shift.

CNN Business is reporting that the housing market is caught in a bind that goes beyond the rate increase itself. Buyer confidence is fragile when oil prices are spiking, gas at the pump is elevated, and the economic outlook carries genuine uncertainty. CNBC notes that the war's effects are already rippling into construction costs, as energy prices push up the price of materials and transportation. That is a double headwind for new housing supply that the market simply does not need right now.

There is a potential tailwind worth flagging. The Federal Open Market Committee held the federal funds rate steady at 3.50 to 3.75 percent at its March meeting. Markets are now beginning to price in a possible cut at the next FOMC meeting, scheduled for April 28th and 29th, particularly if geopolitical risk fades and inflation expectations moderate. A Fed cut does not directly move mortgage rates, but it would send a clear signal that the central bank sees the risk balance shifting toward easing, which typically puts downward pressure on the bond market and, in turn, on fixed rate loan products. For originators and borrowers sitting on the sideline, the next few weeks could represent a meaningful opportunity window if the geopolitical picture improves.

Now to AI, and the story that the technology industry spent most of Tuesday talking about. On March 31st, Anthropic accidentally published internal Claude Code source files to the public npm registry. Specifically, a nearly sixty-megabyte JavaScript source map file, the kind of file normally used only for internal debugging, was inadvertently bundled into version 2.1.88 of the Claude Code package. Security researchers identified it almost immediately. Within hours, the files had been downloaded and mirrored more than 41,500 times on GitHub. Anthropic moved quickly to issue DMCA takedown notices, and GitHub pulled the repositories, but the material was already widely distributed. Bloomberg confirmed the incident Tuesday morning.

What was actually inside? Roughly 512,000 lines of TypeScript, including 44 feature flags representing capabilities that are fully built but not yet shipped to users. The leak also exposed internal model codenames. Capybara refers to a variant of Claude 4.6. Fennec maps to Opus 4.6. And an unreleased model internally called Numbat is apparently still in active testing. Anthropic says no customer data was exposed, and the company attributes the incident to human error in the release pipeline. But the episode raises real questions about supply chain security in AI developer tooling, particularly for enterprises using these platforms in sensitive financial, legal, or operational workflows. The practical risk to most users is low, but the reputational and competitive exposure from a leak of this scale is significant, and the industry will be watching closely how Anthropic manages the aftermath.

On the AI policy front, California Governor Gavin Newsom signed an executive order Monday establishing the state's own framework for AI oversight, directly countering the Trump administration's efforts to restrict state-level AI regulation. The move sets up what could become a significant federal-state collision on AI governance. California is home to Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and most of the major frontier labs, and Newsom's signal is clear: the state intends to set its own standards regardless of what Washington does. Industry observers are watching to see whether other large states align with California's approach or defer to the federal posture.

Meanwhile, OpenAI closed its latest private investment round, drawing capital from Amazon, Nvidia, SoftBank, and Microsoft, among others. Details on the valuation were not immediately disclosed, but the roster of investors signals continued institutional conviction in OpenAI's competitive position, even as the field grows more crowded with serious challengers across multiple fronts. For enterprises evaluating their AI vendor relationships in the wake of this week's Anthropic leak, the investment news is a useful reminder that the frontier lab landscape remains well-capitalized and highly competitive.

Closer to home, let's check in on Columbus. Weather-wise, this is going to be a warm start to April. We are looking at a high around 77 degrees today, with southwesterly winds around 13 miles per hour. The morning and afternoon should be pleasant, but here is the catch: showers and thunderstorms are likely this evening and overnight, with some storms potentially reaching strong-to-severe levels. The Storm Prediction Center has central Ohio in a marginal-to-slight risk category tonight. Get outdoor plans in before dinner, and keep a weather app close as the evening develops.

On the development front, two of the most consequential construction projects in the city's recent history are moving forward in 2026. The Capitol Square Renaissance is the bigger story: a 600-million-dollar redevelopment spanning ten acres in the heart of downtown Columbus. The plans call for more than a thousand new residential units, nine restaurants, nearly 200,000 square feet of office space, and multiple parking decks. If it comes together as designed, it would rank among the most transformative mixed-use projects Columbus has undertaken in a generation. For a downtown that has seen real momentum in recent years, this is the kind of anchor investment that tends to pull additional development behind it.

Just north of downtown, the North Market Merchant Tower is on track to wrap construction this year. The 32-story building will bring a 206-room hotel, more than 170 apartments, 68,000 square feet of office space, and a parking garage to one of the city's most beloved corridors. The North Market has always been an institution, and this tower will bring thousands of new residents and visitors directly into its orbit. NBC4 has been tracking both projects as the central Ohio construction pipeline continues to hum along, even as the national picture gets more complicated.

On the residential side, the Columbus and Central Ohio Regional MLS counted 3,262 closings through the first two months of 2026, essentially flat with the same period a year ago. That stability is worth noting given what is happening nationally. Columbus is outperforming most peer markets, with prices continuing to edge upward despite the rate headwinds from the war. For mortgage professionals in this market, that sustained demand is a foundation to build on when rates eventually ease.

Before I let you go, one thing to watch this week: the Federal Open Market Committee meets April 28th and 29th, and it may be the most consequential Fed meeting in months. After five straight meetings with no change, rate cut speculation is building, particularly if the Iran situation shows signs of genuine resolution. Two data releases between now and then will set the tone. The PCE inflation reading comes out this Friday, and the March jobs report follows next week. If both come in soft, the case for a cut gets substantially stronger, and mortgage rates could see meaningful movement before the end of the month. For anyone in home lending or watching the housing market, those two data points are worth circling on the calendar.

That is your Morning Brief for Wednesday, April 1st. Have a great day. I will see you tomorrow.

item.title: Morning Brief — Tuesday, March 31, 2026
item.description: Your Morning Brief for Tuesday, March 31, 2026. National news, Columbus local, home lending, and AI.
item.content:encoded:
Good morning. It's Tuesday, March 31st, 2026. This is The Morning Brief, your daily rundown of national headlines, Columbus local news, what's moving in home lending, and the latest in AI. I'm your host. We're leading with national news today, where the DHS shutdown is now in its sixth week and the ripple effects are hitting airports hard. Let's get into it.

A quick look at the markets from yesterday's session. The S&P 500 closed at around 6,340, down about half a percent. The Dow edged up slightly, gaining about 50 points to close near 45,216. The Nasdaq had a rougher day, falling roughly one percent. The ten-year Treasury yield dropped sharply to 3.92 percent as investors moved into safer assets amid geopolitical jitters and recession concerns. And the thirty-year fixed mortgage rate is averaging 6.38 percent according to Freddie Mac's latest survey, up from 6.22 percent the week before. That marks the highest level in about six months, and we'll have more on what's driving that in the home lending segment.

The Department of Homeland Security shutdown has now stretched past the six-week mark, and the consequences are becoming impossible to ignore. Roughly 61,000 TSA officers have been working without pay since mid-February, and the strain is showing. Last Friday set a record, with more than 3,500 officers calling out sick, about 12 percent of the entire workforce. Wait times at major airports have ballooned past four hours in some cases, and nearly 500 officers have resigned since the shutdown began. That is a significant talent drain for an agency that was already struggling to recruit. Over the weekend, President Trump signed a memorandum directing DHS to use available funds to start paying TSA employees immediately, with paychecks expected to begin going out yesterday. The money comes from the One Big Beautiful Bill Act passed earlier this year. But here is the catch. The underlying shutdown is not resolved. The House passed a 60-day funding bill, the Senate passed its own version that strips out ICE funding, and both chambers are now in recess until April 13th. So the paychecks may offer some relief, but the political impasse that caused this mess remains firmly in place. If you are flying anytime soon, build in extra time.

Meanwhile, the Treasury Department made a midseason pitch for the administration's tax agenda. Secretary Scott Bessent announced Monday that the Working Families Tax Cuts are, in his words, putting more money back into the pockets of American families, workers, and small business owners. The announcement is timed to the midpoint of the 2026 filing season, when roughly half of all returns are in. The administration is clearly trying to make the tax cuts tangible for ordinary households as filing season makes people unusually aware of what they owe and what they are getting back. Whether voters feel the difference at the kitchen table remains the key political question heading into the fall.

On the economic front, the labor market continues to send mixed signals. The most recent data shows about 130,000 new jobs added, with the unemployment rate holding steady at 4.3 percent. That sounds stable enough on the surface, but zoom out and the picture is more cautious. Annual job growth in 2025 came in at just 181,000 positions for the entire year, the slowest pace outside of a recession on record. The Fed is holding its benchmark rate at 3.5 to 3.75 percent and projecting unemployment to tick up slightly to 4.4 percent by the end of 2026. Inflation is still running around 3 percent, above the Fed's target. Economists are describing this as a low-hire, low-fire environment. Companies are not laying people off in large numbers, but they are not adding headcount aggressively either. It is a labor market that is stable but not exactly inspiring confidence about the road ahead.

And one more national item. The EPA issued a temporary fuel waiver effective May 1st through the 20th that will allow nationwide summer sales of E15 gasoline and suspend federal enforcement of state boutique fuel requirements. The idea is to boost fuel supply and stabilize prices heading into peak summer driving season. If you are planning road trips this summer, this could help keep a few cents off at the pump.

Shifting gears to Columbus and Central Ohio. Weather-wise, Columbus is looking at a high around 73 degrees today with increasing clouds through the afternoon. There is a chance of showers and storms developing later this evening, so keep an umbrella handy if you are heading out after work.

Columbus City Council meets today and will introduce a ceremonial resolution honoring three members of the Ohio Air National Guard who were killed when their KC-135 refueling tanker crashed in Iraq. Master Sergeant Tyler Simmons, 28, and Captain Curtis Angst, 30, were both from Columbus. Captain Seth Koval, 38, was from Stoutsville. The resolution recognizes their service and sacrifice, and it is a somber reminder that Central Ohio's military families continue to bear real costs in conflicts overseas.

On the development and transit front, COTA continues to make moves. The transit authority's board approved nearly 30 million dollars in funding for 37 sidewalk, biking, and trail projects across the region this year. That is on top of the 42 million dollar federal grant COTA secured for the first dedicated transit corridor in Central Ohio. Construction on the Spring Street Terminal renovation continues through May, and a new bus route, Line 30, is now connecting Dublin, Hilliard, Upper Arlington, and Ohio State's campus. For a city that has long been car-dependent, this is a meaningful investment in multimodal infrastructure, and it is worth watching whether ridership numbers follow the money.

And in the housing market, Columbus continues to run hot. The median home price has reached 315,000 dollars, up about 3.3 percent from a year ago. Inventory sits at just 1.6 months, which is firmly in seller's territory. Homes are taking a bit longer to sell, with the median time on market stretching to 49 days from 43 last year, which gives buyers at least a slightly wider window. The demand is being fueled by major employers. Honda, Google, Intel, Amazon, and OhioHealth are all drawing talent to the region, and that population growth is keeping pressure on a housing supply that simply has not kept up.

Now to home lending, where the past few weeks have been a rough ride for anyone watching rates. The thirty-year fixed mortgage rate climbed to 6.38 percent as of last week's Freddie Mac survey, up from 6.22 percent the week before. That represents a jump of roughly 50 basis points since February, when rates briefly dipped below 6 percent for the first time in years. The culprit is a familiar one. Geopolitical tensions, particularly around the situation in Iran, have pushed oil prices higher and reignited inflation fears. Treasury yields spiked, and the mortgage market spread has widened, meaning rates are climbing even faster than underlying bond yields would suggest. Mortgage applications have responded predictably. The MBA reported a 10.5 percent decline in total applications for the week ending March 20th, with refinance applications dropping 15 percent and purchase applications down 5 percent. The spring buying season, which lenders were counting on for a boost, is getting a cold splash of rate reality.

On the regulatory side, a March 13th executive order is drawing significant attention across the industry. President Trump directed the CFPB and FHFA to consider overhauling mortgage regulations for community banks and institutions with under 100 billion dollars in assets. The order calls for tailoring ability-to-repay and qualified mortgage standards for smaller lenders, raising HMDA reporting thresholds to reduce compliance burden, and modernizing appraisal rules to expand the use of desktop appraisals, hybrid appraisals, and AI-powered valuation tools. The order also directed FHFA to lower enterprise housing goals for Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac over the next three years, with the single-family low-income goal dropping from 25 to 21 percent and the very low-income goal nearly cut in half, from 6 to 3.5 percent. This signals a clear tilt toward deregulation and credit expansion through smaller banks, though consumer advocates are raising concerns about what reduced housing goals could mean for underserved communities.

And speaking of technology in mortgage, 2026 is shaping up as a pivotal year for AI adoption in the industry. According to recent surveys, 83 percent of lenders plan to increase their generative AI budgets this year, with documented performance improvements including up to 50 percent increases in origination volume and loan closings happening two and a half times faster. The challenge, as always, is infrastructure. Two-thirds of lenders are using or testing AI, but only a fraction have the digital backbone to scale those tools effectively. The regulatory tailwind from that March executive order, which explicitly encourages AI-powered appraisals, could accelerate adoption further.

And finally, the AI and technology beat, where the headlines have been dominated by a collision between Silicon Valley ethics and Pentagon procurement. The Defense Department designated Anthropic, the maker of Claude, as a supply-chain risk after the company refused to allow military use of its technology for mass surveillance or autonomous weapons. What happened next was remarkable. More than 30 employees from rival companies OpenAI and Google DeepMind, including Google chief scientist Jeff Dean, filed legal briefs defending Anthropic's position. The timing raised eyebrows because the Pentagon signed a deal with OpenAI almost immediately after blacklisting Anthropic, prompting internal backlash at OpenAI itself. This is a defining moment for the industry. The question of whether an AI company can maintain ethical red lines while competing for government contracts worth billions is no longer theoretical. It is playing out in real time, and the fractures it is creating within these companies are significant.

On the policy front, the White House released its National Policy Framework for Artificial Intelligence on March 20th, outlining seven pillars for federal AI governance. The most notable decision is what it does not do. It explicitly instructs Congress not to create a new federal AI regulator. Instead, the framework calls for sector-specific oversight through existing agencies and industry-led standards. It also pushes for broad federal preemption of state AI laws, which would replace the current patchwork of state regulations with a unified national approach. Industry leaders have largely welcomed this as more workable than navigating 50 different state regimes, though critics argue that without a dedicated regulator, enforcement will lack teeth. The framework also prioritizes child safety measures, intellectual property protection, and maintaining American competitiveness in AI development.

And in the enterprise space, AI adoption continues its rapid climb. A new report from ServiceTitan found that 38 percent of commercial contractors now report measurable business impact from AI tools, more than double the 17 percent figure from 2025. Similar acceleration is happening in HR, where 69 percent of recruiting professionals are now using AI and autonomous agents are being embedded directly into major platforms like ADP. The governance gap, though, is real. Only a small fraction of companies deploying AI in hiring have documented oversight processes, leaving them exposed to compliance risks around algorithmic bias. Companies are moving fast, but the guardrails are not keeping pace.

Before I let you go, one thing to watch this week. Congress is in recess until April 13th, which means the DHS shutdown will almost certainly stretch past the seven-week mark. But keep an eye on whether the TSA payment workaround holds up logistically. If those paychecks actually reach officers this week, it could ease the airport chaos. If they do not, expect the political pressure on both parties to intensify significantly when lawmakers return.

That's your Morning Brief for Tuesday. Have a great day. I'll see you tomorrow.

item.title: Morning Brief — Monday, March 30, 2026
